titleStmt.title: An essay on the best means of ascertaining the affinities of oriental languages

monogr.title: Wilhelm von Humboldts Werke. Sechster Band

editorialDecl.p: This digital edition follows the TEI P5 Guidelines.

head.title: An Essay on the best Means of ascertaining the Affinities of Oriental Languages.

div.p: Contained in a Letter addressed to Sir Alexander Johnston, Knt., V.P. R. A.S.
Read June 14, 1828.
Sir:

I have the honour to return 3'ou Sir James Mackintosh's interesting memoir.^) It possesses (like every thing which comes from the pen of that gifted and ingenious writer) the highest interest; and the ideas which are so luminousl}'^ developed in it have the more merit, if we consider, that, at the period when this men:ioir was published, philosophical notions on the study and nature of languages were rarer and more novel than they are at present.

I would, in the first place, observe, that the Royal Asiatic Societ}' could not direct its elForts to a point more important, and more intimately connected with the national glor}^, than that of endeavouring to throw further light on the relations which subsist among the different Indian dialects. Since we cannot doubt that this part of Asia was the cradle of the arts and sciences at an extremel}' remote period, it would be highh^ interesting to ascer- tain with greater certainty whether the Sanscrit be a primitive idiom belonging to those countries, or whether, on the contrary, as most of the learned are at present inclined to believe, it was

Erster Druck: Transactions of the royal asiatic society of Great Britain and
in dialects ditVcring little l'rom each other, and vvhcther we are to assign their origin to thcse islands or to the continent. Mr. Mllis's paper on thc Malayälam langiiagc, with vvhich you were so good as to lurnish me, contains assertions on the aflinity ot" the Tamul language to the idioms of Java, which it would be vcry imponant to verify.

It must be confessed ihat ihese problems are extremely diiiiciilt to solve; and it is probable that we shall never arrive at results which are quite certain: we should, however, carry these re- searches as far as possible, and the difriculty of the undertaking ought not to deter, but rather to induce us to select the most solid and certain means of insuring success. This is more parti- cularlv the point to vvhich I wish to direct your attention, since vou have been pleased to ask my opinion respecting the mcthods proposed by Sir James Mackintosh. It would assuredly have been very desirable to execute his plan, at the period when it was formed; we should then by this time have had more complete information regarding the languages of India; and should perhaps have been in the possession of dialects, of the existence of which we are now ignorant. There do exist, however, some works, such as Sir James calls for. Not to mention printed books, 1 have myself seen in the library of the Kast-lndia Company a manuscript collection of Sanscrit words, comparcd in great numbers with those of the other languages of India, made under the direction of Mr. (]olebrooke. Some distinguished authors, as for instance Mr. Campbell, in his Telugu Dictionary,') have been at pains tc» mark from what foreign idioni such words are derived, as are not proper to the language of which they form a part : and il these works do not embrace all the Indian idioms, they have. on the other hand, the advantage of comprehending entire languages, or at least of not being confined to a limited number of cxpressions. in the present State of our knowledge of the languages of India, which is very ditTerent from that of iXo()., and possessing. as we now do, grammars and dictionaries of most ol these idioms, I
that we can judge of the affinity of languages merely b}' a certain niimber of ideas expressed in the different languages which we wish to compare. I beg you will not suppose, however, that I am insensible to the value and Utility of these comparisons: on the contrary. when they are well executed, I appreciate all their importance; but 1 can never deem them sufficient to answer the end for which they have been undertaken; they certainly form a part of the data to be taken into account in deciding on the affinity of languages, but we should never be guided by them alone, if we wish to arrive at a solid, complete, and certain con- clusion. If we would make ourselves acquainted with the relation which subsists between two languages, we ought to possess a thorough and profound knowledge of each of them. This is a principle dictated alike by common sense and by that precision acquired by the habit of scientific research.

I do not mean to say, that, if we are unable to attain a profound knowledge of each idiom, we should on this account entirely suspend our judgment: I only insist on it that we should not prescribe to ourselves arbitraty limits, and imagine that we are forming our judgment on a firm basis, while it is in reality insufficient.

The method of comparing a certain number of words of one existing language with those of several others has always the two-fold inconvenience of neglecting entirely the grammatical rela- tions, as if the grammar was not as essential a part of the language as the words; and of taking from the language which we wish to examine isolated words, selected, not according to their
mcthods by which each languagc Ibrms its derivatives. The com- parison of two languagcs requircs, that we should examine whether, and in what degree, the roots and derivative terms are common to both. It is not, thcn, by terms expressive of general ideas, such as sun, moon, man, woman cct.^ that we must commcnce the comparison of two languagcs, but by thcir entire dictionar\' critically cxplained. The simple comparison of a certain number of words, by reducing the cxamination of languagcs too much to a mere mechanical labour, often leads us to omit examining sufti- ciently the words which form the subjects of our comparison; and to avoid this defect, we are forced to enter deeph' into all the minutiae of grammar, separating the words from thcir grammatical aftlxcs, and comparing onh' what is really cssential to the expression of the idea which they represent. The words. of which we seek a translation in ditVcrent languagcs, often cannot be rendered except by a Compound term. Thus the sun in some languagcs is called the father, the author, the star cct of day. It is evident, that, in these cases, we no longer compare the same words, but words altogether dilTcrent. To conclude: it is impos- sible to form a correct judgmcnt on the rescmblance of sounds withüut having carefully studied the System of sounds of each of the languagcs which we would compare. There occur often between ditVerent languagcs, and still more frequently between different dialects, regulär transformations of letters, by which we can discovcr the identity of words that at first view seem to have
tellectual faculties of nations, it is ver}^ natural that one generation should transmit theirs to that which follows it; while words, being simple signs of ideas, may be adopted by races altogether distinct. If I attach great importance, however, under this view, to the grammar of a language, I do not refer to the S3'stem of grammar in general, but to grammatical forms, considered with respect to their System and their sounds taken conjointly.

If two languages, such for instance as the Sanscrit and the Greek, exhibit grammatical forms which are identical in arrange- ment, and have a close analogy in their sounds, we have an in- contestable proof that these two languages belong to the same family.

If, on the contrar}^, two languages do contain a great number of words in common, but have no grammatical identit}', their affinity becomes a matter of great doubt; and if their grammars have, like those of the Basque and the Latin, an essentially different character, these two languages certainly do not belong to the same family. The words of the one have been merely trans- planted into the other, which has nevertheless retained its primi- tive forms.

If I assert that, in order to prove the affinity of languages, we should pay attention to the employment of grammatical forms and to their sounds taken together, it is because l would affirm that the}^ must be considered not only in the abstract, but in the concrete. Some examples will render this clearer.

Several American languages have two plural forms in the first person, an exclusive and an inclusive form, according as we would include or exclude the person addressed. It has been thought that this peculiarity belonged exclusively to the American languages; but it is also found in the Mantchu, the Tamul, and in all the dialects of the South Sea Islands. All these languages have indeed this grammatical form in common; but it is only in the abstract. Each of them expresses it by a different sound : the
////// and ///, as in jctiikämn^ „dcsirous of vanqiiishing", corrcspond as grammatical forms with thc Latin supincs; and therc is at thc same time a perfcct idcntity of soiind in thcsc l'orms in the two languages, as the Latin supincs tcrminate invariably in tum and tu. The striking conformity of thc Sanscrit aiixiliary verb to that of the Greek and Lithuanian languagcs has bccn ingeniously developed by Professor Bopp. Thc Sanscrit -ivcda, thc (ircek olda., and the (jothic vaü are evidently of thc same origin. In all thcse thrce words thcre is a conformity both of sound and signification ; biit fiirther: all thc thrcc verbal forms have thcse two peculiaritics in common, that, thoiigh preterites, they are used in a prcscnt sense, and that in all thrce the short radical vowel, which is retained in the plural, is changed to a long vowel in the singular. The Lithuanian iveizdmi^ I k n o w^ , and the Sanscrit ivedmi shew clearly at rirst view that this word is not only the same in thc two languagcs (as bos and bccf in Latin and English), but that the two languagcs have, in the tcrmination ;///, modcUcd thcse words on thc same grammatical form; for they not only mark the persons of the verb by inflexions added to the end of the root, but the affix of the first person singular is in both cases the syllable vii.

Thcre is then in the examples adduced a conformity in grammatical use, and at the same time in sound; and it is impossiblc to deny that the languagcs which possess thcsc forms must be of thc same family.

The dilVcrcnce between the real affinity of languagcs, whicii presumes a tiliation as it were among thc nations who speak them, and that degree of relation which is purcly historical, and only indicatcs tcmporary and accidcntal conncxions among nations, is, in my opinion, of thc grcatest importancc. Xow it appears to mc impossiblc ever to ascertain that ditlcrencc mcrcly by thc examination of words; espccially, if wc cxaminc but a small numbcr of thcm.
bold, when expressed in this gcneral vvay; yet I am strongly in- clined to consider it correct, provided the expression gram mar be not taken vaguely, but with a due regard to the sounds of grammatical forms. But whatever opinion may be entertained with respect to this manner of considering the dilTerence of languages, it appears to me at all events demonstrated :

B'irst, that all research into the aftinity of languages, which does not enter quite as much into the examination of the grammatical System as into that of words, is faulty and imperfect; and, Secondly, that the proofs of the real affinity of languages, that is to say, the question whether two languages belong to the same family, ought to be principally deduced from the gramma- tical System, and can be deduced from that alone; since the identity of words only proves a resemblance such as may be purely historical and accidental.

Sir James Mackintosh rejects the examination of grammar, for this reason, that languages which are evidently of the same stock have ver}^ different grammars. But we must not be misled by this phenomenon, although it is in itself quite true. The grammatical form of languages depends, on the one hand, it is true, upon the nature of these languages ; but it also depends, on the other hand, upon the changes which they experience in the course of ages, and in consequence of historical revolutions. Out of these changes it has arisen, that languages of the same family have a different grammatical system, and that languages really distinct resemble each other in some degree. But the slightest examination will suffice to shew the real relations which subsist between those languages, especially if by following the plan above laid down we proceed to the examination of forms which are alike identical in their uses and in their sounds. It is thus that we discover without difficulty that thc English language is of Germanic origin, and that the Persian belongs to the Sanscrit family of languages, notwiihstanding the ver}'^ great dilference which exists between the grammars of these Idioms.

It is generally believed, that the aftinity of two languages is undeniably proved, if words that are applied to objects which must have bcen known to the natives ever since their existence,
judging ol' thc uflinity <>f languagcs seems m mc by no means infalliblc. It ölten happens, that cven the objccts of our carliesi perccptions, or of thc lirst nccessity, are rcprcscnicd by words taken from foreign languagcs, and which belong to a dillcrent class. If \ve only examine the list furnished by Sir James Mackin- tosh, WC shall lind thcrc such words as people, countenance, touch, voice, labour, force, power, marriagc, spirit, circle, tempest, autumn, time, mountain, valle}', air, vapour, herb, verdure, and othcrs of the samc kind. Xow all these words being evidcntly derived from the Latin, as it was transformcd after the fall of the Roman empire, we ought, judging from these words, rather to assign to the English an origin simiiar to that of the Roman languagcs than to that of thc German.

If what I have herc advanccd bc well foundcd, it appcars to me easy to point out thc System which thc Royal Asiatic Society would do well to pursuc, in order to complctc our knowlcdgc of the Indian languagcs, and to resolve thc grand problem which they present to the minds of philologists who endeavour to dis- covcr the origin and the filiation of languagcs.

It would be proper to commence by examining the country geographically, taking a review of even' part of India, in order to know cxactly in what parts we are still in want of suftkient materials to determine the nature of their idioms. Where deti- ciencies are discovered, elforts should be used for their supply, by encouraging those persons who are alread}' employed on those languagcs, or may intend studying them, to form grammars and dictionaries, and to publish the principal works existing in these languagcs, for which every facility should be alTorded them. If materials to a ccrtain extent wcre thus collectcd, wc should un- qucsiionably not want men who would be able to deduce from them conclusions from which to prepare a critical vicw of the aftinity of the Indian languagcs, and to determine, as far as the data which we might possess would admit, thc manncr in which the Sanscrit and other languagcs of India and its islands have reciprocally acted upon each other. I assume that the Icarncd of the (kontinent would take their share in this work, Monsieur
of bcing able to direct works upon these languagcs to be under- taken in India itself, and to guide such labours by plans sent from this country. In India these are living languages, and literary men of the very nations in which they are spoken may be employed in the researches we wish to forward. No other nation possesses so valuable an advantage. It is important to protit by it. The delkiencies in our knowledge are numerous and evident. We possess scarcel}^ any thing upon the Malayalim, and are in want of a printed dictionary of the Tamul. But while we keep this object strictly in view, and work upon a fixed plan, we shall insensibly fill up these vacancies. It is certainly difticult to find men who both can and will engage in a work like this, but the}' are undoubtedly to be found. Thus Dr. Babing- ton has mentioned Mr. Whish to me, as being profoundly acquain- ted with the Malayalim, and as being already employed in making it better known in Europe. Solid labours upon languages are, in their nature, slow. In an enterprize so vast as that of exa- mining to the utmost possible extent each of the numerous languages of India, progress can only be made insensibly and step by Step. But learned societies afford this advantage, that the same labour can be continued through a long series of years; and com- plete and perfect works upon two or three idioms are certainly preferable to notions, more or less superficial, upon all the dia- lects of India, hastily put forth for the purpose of Coming at once to a general conclusion.

These, Sir, are my ideas upon the subject, upon which you wished to have my opinion. It is only in compliance with your request, that I have ventured to lay them before you; for I am well aware how much better able the distinguished members of the Royal Asiatic Society are to form a judgment of, and give an opinion upon this matter than I am.
I request you, Sir, to accept the assurance of my highest respect.
(Signed) Humboldt. London, June lo, 1828.
